The white crust ahead is split by hairline cracks that do not match the wind. When the party crosses, a giant scorpion erupts from the hardpan with claws already raised.
Shapes waver beside a line of ruined stones, vanishing whenever the wind rises. The bandits wait until the glare hides their drawstrings.
Giant vultures hop along the rim of a dry wash, bold from hunger and carrion. They take wing only long enough to dive at anything that looks weak.
The dunes have erased every trail except a line of wind-carved ridges. Reading their shadows points toward water before the sun moves too far.
A black ridge of sun-baked stone radiates heat through boots and gloves. Crossing it quickly hurts less than stopping halfway.
A city of green towers trembles on the horizon, perfect and unreachable. It fades into ordinary dust when approached, leaving only a cooler breath of wind.
Only the top of a carved route marker shows above a dune. Digging it free reveals an old warning and the direction of a road the desert swallowed.
Faint camel bells ring from beyond a ridge where no caravan can be seen. The sound follows the party until someone chooses to answer it.
A sun-black obelisk has split down the middle, and the shadow inside points the wrong way. Locals want it sealed before nightfall.
Red fruit grows high on a many-spined cactus, sweet enough to be worth the trouble. The ripest pieces sit where the spines are densest.
A fan of bitter aloe hides in the shade of a cracked boulder. Clean cuts preserve the gel before the leaves dry in the wind.
Dry pods rattle beneath low scrub where the sand holds a trace of moisture. The seeds are small, filling, and easily lost to the wind.
Small fish hide under reeds in a shaded oasis pool. Nets must be worked gently so the mud does not foul the only clean water nearby.
A rain-cut wadi still holds a deep pocket of brown water beneath overhanging stone. Catfish wait at the bottom where the pool is coolest.
A shallow salt pool flickers with tiny brine shrimp after rare rain. The harvest is quick work before the sun thickens the water again.
Green stains mark a copper seam exposed along a dry cliff face. The rock is brittle, so controlled strikes matter more than force.
Wind has scraped a salt pan into clean geometric plates. The best slabs lift whole if separated before the edge crumbles.
Golden pebbles glitter in the bed of a vanished stream. Sorting true sunstone from common quartz takes patience in the glare.
A dead acacia limb has seasoned hard in the open air. Its thorns make the work slow, but the heartwood burns hot and clean.
A palm has fallen beside the oasis with its trunk still mostly sound. Cutting it without fouling the water requires careful staging.
A half-buried cargo crate creaks beneath the sand, its owner long gone. The planks can be salvaged if the shifting dune is braced first.
